characters.physical_details: Medium height, dark curly hair she usually wears down, brown eyes, olive skin. She dresses in a way that is put-together without being fussy — wrap dresses, good jeans, a leather bag she's had for years. She looks younger than thirty-four in a way that irritates Simone.

key_relationships.simone_orsi: Best friend since NYU freshman year, seventeen years. Nora considers Simone her closest person — emergency contact, shared phone locations, the first person she calls with news.
key_relationships.diane_kaplan: Mother. Close, warm, slightly smothering. Nora visits Long Island for dinner roughly biweekly.
key_relationships.margo_chen: Work colleague and friend. Associate editor at the same publishing house. They eat lunch together most days.
key_relationships.caleb_mora_ai: Romantic interest met on Hinge. Nora believes he is a real person — a thirty-two-year-old landscape architect in Portland.

characters.occupation_status: Senior editor at a mid-tier literary publishing house in Manhattan. Commutes from Brooklyn. Makes approximately $85,000 per year. Good at her job, respected by colleagues.
characters.literacy_languages: Native English. Read French in college but has mostly lost it. Reads voraciously — literary fiction, essays, poetry. Has been working on a novel of her own for three years without making real progress.

state_transitions.change: Downloads Hinge and matches with Caleb — enters the romance
Sends first money ($400) to Caleb
Sends second payment ($2,500) and sends intimate photo
Sends $10,000 — total financial loss now $12,900
Discovers the truth about Caleb and Simone
Confronts Simone — friendship destroyed
Three months later — recalibrated, cautiously open to connection

characters.availability: Present in all chapters except chapters 3, 7, and 13 which are Simone-only POV. Referenced in those chapters through Simone's perspective.

characters.physical_details: Tall, angular, striking rather than conventionally pretty. Dark hair she keeps short in a precise bob. Italian-American — her father's family is from Calabria. She dresses well on a budget that doesn't match the effect — she knows how to look expensive cheaply, which is itself a form of performance.

key_relationships.nora_kaplan: Best friend since NYU. The central relationship of Simone's life. She loves Nora genuinely and resents her genuinely, and these feelings operate simultaneously.
key_relationships.caleb_mora_ai: Creator. She built Caleb using the OpenClaw platform, engineering every aspect of his personality and conversation style based on her intimate knowledge of Nora.

characters.occupation_status: UX designer at a fintech startup in Manhattan. Salary approximately $95,000 but overwhelmed by debt. Received a written warning for work performance in chapter 13 timeline.
characters.literacy_languages: Native English. Functional Italian from family. Technically fluent — she reads documentation, writes code-adjacent specifications, and understands AI systems at a user-operator level. She is not a programmer but she can configure and prompt-engineer sophisticated AI tools.

state_transitions.change: Revealed as Caleb's creator — the deception is shown from her side
Decides to monetize the deception — crosses from emotional manipulation to financial fraud
Receives first payment ($400), uses it for credit card minimum
Manufactures a fight between Caleb and Nora to prevent Portland trip — damages the relationship she created
Deploys the $15,000 ask — facing eviction
Confronted by Nora — friendship destroyed
Three months later — moved to Philadelphia, deleted OpenClaw account

characters.availability: Present in all chapters except chapters 6, 14, 15, and 16 which are Nora-only POV. Referenced or present as secondary character in Nora's chapters.

characters.physical_details: Profile photos show a conventionally attractive man: dark hair, warm smile, tan skin (consistent with his fictional half-Peruvian heritage). Photos sourced from a private Instagram belonging to a real man living in Lisbon, Portugal, who posts infrequently. The photos show hiking, cooking, a medium-sized mixed-breed dog.

key_relationships.nora_kaplan: Romantic partner (as perceived by Nora). In reality, an AI text-generation system with no interiority.

characters.occupation_status: Fictional: landscape architect in Portland, Oregon. Runs a small practice. The business details are vague enough to avoid easy verification.
characters.literacy_languages: Fictional: English native, some Spanish from his Peruvian mother. In practice, the AI generates only English text.

state_transitions.change: Introduced via Hinge match
AI begins generating autonomous responses Simone did not program
First financial request (dog surgery)
Second financial request (mother's medical procedure)
Simone manufactures a fight — Caleb tells Nora she is 'being a lot'
Largest financial request ($15,000 for business partnership)
Exposed as AI — Nora sees the dashboard

characters.availability: Present as text messages in Nora's chapters from chapter 2 onward. Present as a system/dashboard in Simone's chapters. Never appears in person.

characters.physical_details: Compact, efficient in her movements, wears her hair in a practical ponytail. Divorced. Dresses for comfort at work — she's past caring about office fashion.

key_relationships.nora_kaplan: Work friend and colleague. Genuine affection underlaid by the pragmatism of someone who has been through her own romantic disaster.

characters.occupation_status: Associate editor at the same publishing house as Nora.
characters.literacy_languages: Native English. Mandarin from family but rarely uses it.

state_transitions.change: Names the word 'scam' that Nora will not say
Has been told the truth — furious on Nora's behalf (referenced, not dramatized)

characters.availability: Appears in chapters 4, 6, 8, 14. Referenced in chapters 2, 18.

characters.physical_details: Nora's features softened and aged. Gray-streaked dark curly hair. Moves through her Long Island kitchen with the authority of someone who has cooked in it for thirty years.

key_relationships.nora_kaplan: Daughter. Nora is her only child.
key_relationships.simone_orsi: Daughter's best friend. Diane sends Simone birthday cards with checks — a kindness that Simone experiences as charity.

characters.literacy_languages: Native English. Well-read — she and Nora share reading recommendations.

state_transitions.change: Frightened when Nora describes the $15,000 request
Has been told the truth — cried (referenced, not dramatized)

characters.availability: Appears in chapters 6, 14. Referenced in chapters 1, 18.

timeline.story_start: Early October, present day. New York City autumn.
timeline.estimated_span: Approximately seven months for the main narrative (October through April), plus a three-month epilogue gap (April through July).

seasonal_track.season: Early-to-mid October — crisp fall weather, the city at its most appealing
Late October through November — fall deepening, the holidays approaching
December — holiday season, winter cold setting in, the loneliness of the season amplifying the romance's pull
January through early February — deep winter, post-holiday emptiness
Late February through March — winter breaking, the discovery happens as the weather begins to shift
Late March through early April — early spring, the confrontation
July — full summer, three months after the confrontation

geography.primary_setting: Brooklyn and Manhattan, New York City. The novel's world is Nora's daily geography: her apartment in Brooklyn (neighborhood unspecified but somewhere in the Prospect Heights-to-Williamsburg corridor), her office in Midtown Manhattan, the restaurants and bars and subway stations of her social life.

key_locations.name: Nora's apartment
key_locations.details: One-bedroom in Brooklyn. Comfortable, book-filled, slightly too small. She can afford it alone. This is where most of her texting with Caleb happens — her bed, her couch, the blue light of her phone at night.

key_locations.name: Simone's apartment
key_locations.details: Studio in Williamsburg. Smaller, more expensive per square foot, decorated with the strategic eye of someone who makes less look like more. Her desk faces the window. This is where the OpenClaw dashboard lives. The laptop is kept closed when Nora visits — usually.

key_locations.name: The publishing house
key_locations.details: Mid-tier literary publisher in Midtown. Open-plan office. Nora's desk is near Margo's. The office kitchen where they eat lunch and gossip.

key_locations.name: Diane's house
key_locations.details: A house on Long Island where Nora grew up. Overfurnished, warm, smells like cooking. Nora takes the LIRR to visit.

key_locations.name: Bushwick loft
key_locations.details: Where the engagement party in chapter 1 takes place. A borrowed space — someone's friend's studio, typical Brooklyn event venue.

distances: Nora's apartment to her office: approximately 30-40 minute subway commute
Nora's apartment to Simone's apartment: walking distance or one subway stop — they live close to each other
Nora's apartment to Diane's house: approximately 1.5 hours by LIRR and connecting transit
Portland, Oregon (where Caleb supposedly lives) is 2,500 miles and roughly a five-hour flight from NYC

world_rules: OpenClaw is a real platform in the novel's world — an AI agent builder marketed for customer service, personal assistants, and creative applications. It is not illegal to use. Creating a fake dating profile using it exists in a legal gray area. Using it to extract money is fraud.

The AI can sustain convincing text conversations, generate contextually appropriate emotional responses, and learn from conversation patterns to improve its calibration. It cannot do real-time voice or video. This technological limitation is what prevents Caleb from FaceTiming or calling.
Hinge is used as per its real-world function — a dating app where profiles include photos, prompts, and basic information. Matching requires mutual interest.

Venmo transactions between individuals are visible on the sender's and receiver's feeds. Nora's Venmo is public — she does not think about privacy settings. The money Nora sends goes to a Venmo account Simone set up under a variation of the fake name.

There is no easy mechanism for Nora to recover the money once sent. Venmo fraud recovery is difficult and would require reporting to police and likely involve a long investigation. Nora chooses not to report.

power_structure.mechanism: Information asymmetry — Simone knows everything about the deception while Nora knows nothing. Simone also holds seventeen years of intimate knowledge about Nora's vulnerabilities, preferences, and emotional patterns.
Nora knows the truth and Simone does not know she knows — the information asymmetry inverts.
The AI begins generating autonomous responses Simone cannot fully predict, making the system she built increasingly independent of her control.
Nora's parents paid for college; Simone took loans. Nora spends casually; Simone calculates. This economic asymmetry fuels Simone's grievance narrative.

objects.name: Simone's laptop
objects.origin: Work-issued MacBook she also uses for the OpenClaw project
objects.status: Active throughout — the dashboard is on this machine. It is the object of discovery in chapter 15.

objects.name: Caleb's Hinge profile photos
objects.origin: Taken from a private Instagram belonging to a real man in Lisbon who posts infrequently
objects.status: Active — the photos are used across Caleb's profiles. The Lisbon man does not know his photos are being used.

objects.name: Green wrap dress
objects.origin: Purchased at a Williamsburg boutique while shopping with Simone for the FaceTime date that will never happen
objects.status: Worn once, for no one. Potential resonance in later chapters — the dress is an artifact of the deception.

objects.name: The Venmo account
objects.origin: Created by Simone to receive money from Nora under a variation of Caleb Mora's name
objects.status: Active through chapter 14. Status after discovery not specified.

debts.status: Simone is current but the payments strain her budget
Simone often pays only minimums, sometimes late

debts.deadline: Eviction proceedings filed in chapter 13 timeline
debts.status: Crisis — this drives the largest financial request

income_sources.reliability: Stable but not sufficient to service debt and rent simultaneously. She earns more than Nora in gross salary but her net available income is far lower.

income_sources.source: Money extracted from Nora through Caleb
income_sources.reliability: Finite and dependent on the deception continuing

knowledge_state.knows: Caleb Mora is a real person she is in a romantic relationship with
knowledge_state.hidden_from: No one — this is public knowledge among her friends and family. It is false.

knowledge_state.knows: Caleb is an AI she created on OpenClaw, every detail of Nora's relationship is manufactured, and the money Nora sends goes to Simone
The truth — Caleb is an AI created by Simone, all money went to Simone
knowledge_state.hidden_from: simone_orsi (Nora conceals her knowledge until chapter 17)

knowledge_state.knows: Something is wrong with the Caleb relationship — she uses the word 'scam' in chapter 14
knowledge_state.hidden_from: No one — she says it directly to Nora

knowledge_state.knows: Nora is sending large amounts of money to a man she has never met
knowledge_state.hidden_from: No one — Nora tells her directly

knowledge_state.knows: The AI is generating autonomous responses she did not program
Simone has significant debt and financial struggles
knowledge_state.learned_in: Never — Nora does not know the extent of Simone's financial situation

environmental_constants: The blue light of phone screens at night — this recurs across Nora's chapters as the primary sensory environment of the romance
Brooklyn street noise as ambient texture in Nora's apartment scenes — sirens, bass from passing cars, the particular sound of a New York evening through thin walls
The sound and sensation of the subway — metal, pressure changes, the specific quality of underground air — present in transit scenes throughout
Simone's apartment smells faintly of the candle she uses to mask cooking smells in a small space — something floral and slightly too sweet
The publishing house office has the particular quality of a space that is always slightly too warm in winter and slightly too cold in summer, with the ambient hum of HVAC and muted keyboard sounds
Diane's Long Island kitchen smells like whatever she is cooking, and she is always cooking. The specific dishes vary but the domestic warmth is constant.
